The risen Christ stands before His followers, and their first instinct is to ask Him the wrong question. After forty days of appearing to them with convincing proofs of His resurrection, after speaking to them about the kingdom of God, they ask whether He will now restore the kingdom to Israel. It is a deeply human response—they want to know the timeline, to see the plan unfold according to their hopes. But Jesus does not answer their question. Instead, He redirects them entirely. They do not need to know times or seasons; they need to receive power. They do not need to watch the sky; they need to become His witnesses. What looks like refusal is actually an act of mercy. Jesus will not allow His people to become fixated on prophetic details while missing their actual calling. The Ascension itself becomes the visual sermon: your King is going away, and your task is not to stand gazing upward but to go forward into all the earth, empowered by the Holy Spirit.

Yet notice what happens in the immediate aftermath. The disciples return to Jerusalem and gather in prayer—not to analyze what Jesus meant, not to strategize their mission, but to wait. There is something countercultural about this obedience. A hundred and twenty believers locked in a room, praying, doing nothing else. Modern Christianity often struggles to distinguish between waiting on God and wasting time. But these early disciples understood that obedience to Christ's command to wait was itself their work. They do not rush ahead. They do not assume they know what the gift of the Holy Spirit will look like. They simply pray, together, in unity, with the women, with Jesus's own mother and brothers. This is the church before Pentecost—imperfect, confused about the future, but fundamentally united in submission to Christ's word.

The Judas interlude cuts through any romantic notion of this moment. Peter stands up and addresses a mathematical problem: one of the Twelve is gone, cast into his place by his own hand. His death is described with brutal honesty—no euphemism, no softening. This is what betrayal looks like in its end. And yet even here, Scripture is at work. Even this tragedy does not surprise God or fall outside His plan. The disciples search the Old Testament and find that David spoke of this moment centuries before. They cast lots to choose a replacement, praying, "Lord, You know everyone's heart." It is a haunting phrase. God knows what happened with Judas. God knows the hearts of Joseph and Matthias now before them. The disciples are not making this choice blindly; they are making it in full acknowledgment of their own blindness, asking God to see what they cannot.

Acts 1 leaves us in a strange in-between. The Ascension has happened. The Resurrection is real. The kingdom has not yet come in fullness. The Spirit has not yet fallen. The church gathers, prays, waits, and chooses. It is not the dramatic moment we typically celebrate, but it is the moment that defines what the church actually is: a community called to obedience, gathered around Scripture, dependent on prayer, trusting that God knows what He is doing even when we do not. Jesus has given His final command. Now everything depends on whether we will wait for His power rather than act on our own understanding.
